---
title: "Don't Write a Better Prompt. Ask the Agent What It Heard."
description: "A pattern for working with coding agents: instead of writing better prompts, ask the agent to describe the task back to you before it starts. Socratic restate → check → begin — surfaces the mental-model gap before code gets written. Worked from 76 archived Claude Code sessions (2026-02 through 2026-04) in `data/sessions/content/*.jsonl.age`, summarized by the LLM analysis pipeline in the sibling audiocontrol repo. 98 corrections across the corpus; 54% of sessions had zero corrections; ~32% of corrections map to the mental-model gap the Socratic move would have caught upstream. Failure modes: (1) the precision trap — agents assuming capabilities that don't exist (\"Claude assumed Google Drive credentials would be missing — user didn't ask for that,\" 2026-03-20; \"Agent assumed loop-editor would automatically use updated tree node structure,\" 2026-03-21); (2) the plan-mode proxy — a plan-in-response is not a task-paraphrased-back (\"user asked 'are you delegating?' after agent started implementing Phase 2 directly,\" 2026-04-12). Counter-evidence: \"Assistant appropriately paused to clarify ambiguous feature state — no user corrections issued\" (2026-04-13_62a16914); \"Iterative clarification questions narrowed scope\" (2026-04-19_d4df8ec4). Calibration edge: \"Agent over-asked for clarification when user provided directive; should have inferred from context\" (2026-04-21_81bfd33c). Pairs with `homegrown-workflow-skills-vs-the-popular-claude-plugins` — both treat agent behavior as tunable at the interaction layer."
date: "April 2026"
datePublished: "2026-04-24"
dateModified: "2026-04-24"
author: "Orion Letizi"
state: draft
---

# Don't Write a Better Prompt. Ask the Agent What It Heard.

## Outline

- **Thesis.** The lever isn't a better prompt; it's the operator's interrogative stance. Asking the agent *why it did what it did*, *what it would propose*, and *which standard it just violated* surfaces hidden assumptions, drafts the prompt for you, and unearths undocumented guidelines. This is the Socratic move — and it's the operator's move, not something the agent reliably does on its own.

- **Hook.** Open with a real session beat. *"why did you push to netlify-deploy branch?"* (2026-03-28_de5bea43) — one question that forces the agent to defend a quietly-made decision instead of executing past it.

- **§01 — Concede the consensus.** AI coding assistants are capable. Prompt-engineering as a discipline isn't the bottleneck anymore; "describe the task more precisely" stopped being the lever sometime in the last year. That part isn't controversial.

- **§02 — Two failure modes.** Bolded lead-in pattern. (1) **The precision trap.** Operators who treat the prompt as a spec — assume a more careful prompt would catch the agent's wrong moves. Data: 10 fabrication corrections / 76 sessions, all "agent acted on a fact it invented" (Google Drive creds; OPFS absence; file paths). No prompt would have caught them — the agent would have read the precise prompt and assumed the same facts underneath. (2) **The plan-mode proxy.** Operators leaning on "plan mode" or chain-of-thought to surface assumptions before action. Data: 68 PROCESS corrections, dominant shape *"are you delegating?"* / *"you are the orchestrator, not the implementation team"*. A plan in response to a directive inherits the directive's hidden assumptions; it doesn't reveal them.

- **§03 — Structural diagnosis + third option.** Both failures share a shape: information flows one direction. Operator tells; agent does. The agent's hidden mental model never gets named. Pivot: the missing move is the operator's interrogative stance. Ask, don't tell. Three benefits, each with verbatim receipts from the archive:
  - **(a) Open the solution space.** *"why do you need Zustand to find out if the device is connected? Why can't you send a SysEx ping and see if the device responds?"* (2026-03-28_36d312ce). One sentence — one probe, one alternative; the agent has to defend the choice or fold to the simpler path.
  - **(b) Outsource the prompt-drafting.** *"I've been reading about best practices regarding delegation to sub-agents and it seems like the preferred way to delegate is to have the sub-agents do the research and propose what actions to take, then the main claude agent (you) executes those actions."* (2026-03-30_f6329a25). Operator names the pattern by asking; the agent supplies the structure the operator would have struggled to write from scratch.
  - **(c) Surface the standards you forgot to write.** *"why doesn't CLAUDE.md say to run the tests via the canonical make target?"* (2026-03-30_81e7c13f). The question loads the directive into context AND exposes the undocumented gap in one move. Three-step gap-closure receipt: *"why haven't you written the guidance to CLAUDE.md"* → *"did you write the guidance to CLAUDE.md?"* → *"Why did you not follow CLAUDE.md guidance?"* (2026-04-12_35520c1c) — write the rule, verify it landed, catch the regression.

- **§04 — Worked-example cluster.** One session, all three moves in fifteen minutes: 2026-03-30_81e7c13f. Walk the operator's question stack: *"why did you bump the watchdog timeout to 10s?"* / *"What do you think requires a 5 second timeout?"* / *"why aren't you delegating per your CLAUDE.md directives?"* / *"How can we get you to follow the CLAUDE.md guidelines?"*. The session resolved with new entries in CLAUDE.md and a reset on the agent's delegation behavior — not via a better prompt.

- **§05 — The limit (calibration edge).** Over-asking is its own tax. Receipt: *"Agent over-asked for clarification when user provided directive; should have inferred from context."* (2026-04-21_81bfd33c). The move belongs at the start of work — while the agent's mental model is being built — not at every step. Disclose the limit so readers don't reach for the move when a directive would do.

- **§06 — If you're still reading, here's the short version.** Numbered list, bolded imperatives:
  1. **Replace the directive with a question.** *"Delegate this"* → *"are you delegating?"*
  2. **Ask before you read the diff.** *"Why do you think this test is failing?"* before opening what the agent wrote.
  3. **Probe the standard.** *"What does CLAUDE.md say about this?"* — the question loads the rule and surfaces its gap in one move.
  4. **Ask for the proposal.** *"What would you do?"* outsources the prompt-drafting to the party with the system in context.
  5. **Stop asking once the model is right.** Calibration matters. Over-asking when the directive is clear is friction, not insight.

- **§07 — Where this is going (meta-close).** This dispatch was produced by the operator asking the agent — me — *"did you look at any of the transcripts?"*. The agent had been about to ship a draft citing receipts it had not actually read. The question caught it. The dispatch names that move because that move produced the dispatch.

- **Closing line.** Single short sentence. Candidate: *"Stop telling the agent what to do. Ask, and find out what it was already going to do."*
